questionText: The curve $C$ has equation $y = e^x \sec x$ for $-\dfrac{\pi}{2} < x < \dfrac{\pi}{2}$. (a) Show that $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = e^x \sec x\,(1 + \tan x)$. (b) Find the equation of the tangent to $C$ at the point where $x = 0$. (c) Find the exact coordinates of the stationary point of $C$. (d) Determine the nature of this stationary point.

steps.description: Identify the factors for the product rule
steps.explanation: The function is a product, so we use $\tfrac{d}{dx}(uv)=u'v+uv'$. Recall the reciprocal-trig result $\tfrac{d}{dx}\sec x=\sec x\tan x$.
The exponential is its own derivative, and $\sec x$ differentiates to $\sec x\tan x$ (a standard result from applying the quotient/chain rule to $1/\cos x$).
Substituting into $u'v+uv'$ gives the two terms.
Both terms share the factor $e^x\sec x$; taking it out gives the required form and isolates the part that can be zero. This proves (a).
Using $\sec 0=1$ and $\tan 0=0$, the slope of the tangent at the $y$-axis is $1$.
The curve crosses the $y$-axis at height $1$, which is where the tangent touches.
Stationary points need a zero gradient, so we set the factorised derivative to zero.
On this interval $e^x$ is always positive and $\sec x=\tfrac{1}{\cos x}$ can never be zero, so only $1+\tan x$ can vanish.

steps.description: Solve for x in the interval
steps.explanation: On $-\tfrac{\pi}{2}<x<\tfrac{\pi}{2}$ the only solution of $\tan x=-1$ is $x=-\tfrac{\pi}{4}$.

steps.description: Set up a sign test for the gradient
steps.explanation: Both $e^x>0$ and $\sec x>0$ here (as $\cos x>0$), so the sign of the gradient follows $1+\tan x$.

steps.description: Examine the sign either side of the point
steps.explanation: Just left of $-\tfrac{\pi}{4}$ the gradient is negative and just right it is positive.
The gradient turns from negative to positive, so the stationary point is a local minimum with value $\sqrt2\,e^{-\pi/4}$.
